quick_facts.label: Award and level
quick_facts.value: MA (Master of Arts) is a master’s-level postgraduate degree. UGC’s degree-specification notification lists master’s degrees as requiring a bachelor’s degree for entry; the exact MA subject and university rules determine the programme title and requirements.

quick_facts.label: Standard duration
quick_facts.value: The conventional route is generally two years/four semesters after a three-year bachelor’s degree. Under the National Credit Framework and NEP-linked structures, a one-year master’s route may also be offered after a four-year bachelor’s degree (Honours/Research), where the university provides it.

quick_facts.label: Entry qualification
quick_facts.value: UGC’s formal-education master’s regulations state that admission requires a completed three-year undergraduate degree, prescribed undergraduate credits, or an equivalent qualification recognized by the concerned university. Programme-specific subject and marks requirements can be narrower.

quick_facts.label: Curriculum
quick_facts.value: The academic content depends on the MA discipline and university. As an official example, Delhi University’s MA History is structured over four semesters with core, elective, and open-elective coursework; it requires 80 credits in that programme.

quick_facts.label: Illustrative distance-learning cost
quick_facts.value: Fees are institution- and subject-specific. IGNOU lists MA (Urban Studies) at ₹8,200 per year plus a ₹300 registration fee, with examination fees extra; this is an illustrative 2026 programme fee, not a benchmark for all MA degrees.

quick_facts.label: Research and teaching pathway
quick_facts.value: An MA can meet the master’s-degree route to PhD admission when the candidate has at least 55% aggregate or an equivalent grade, subject to the institution’s admission process and applicable category relaxation. For UGC-NET June 2026, the published qualifying threshold is 55% for General/Unreserved/General-EWS master’s candidates and 50% for listed reserved categories.

sections.heading: Eligibility for an MA
paragraphs: At the national regulatory baseline, a student must have completed a three-year undergraduate degree, earned the undergraduate credits prescribed for the degree, or hold an equivalent qualification recognized by the university to enter a master’s programme. Universities then set the actual MA programme rules, including accepted bachelor’s disciplines, minimum marks, reservation, and any entrance-test requirement.

An applicant should therefore read the eligibility table for the exact subject and university rather than assume that a BA automatically guarantees eligibility for every MA specialization. For example, IGNOU’s MA (Urban Studies) accepts graduation in any discipline, while other MA programmes may specify an allied or relevant subject background.
bullets: Confirm that the bachelor’s degree and marks meet the chosen university’s published MA eligibility.
Check whether the programme accepts any bachelor’s discipline or requires a relevant/allied subject.
If applying through a reserved category, verify the university’s current category, certificate, and relaxation rules.

sections.heading: Duration and study format
paragraphs: For the standard route after a three-year bachelor’s degree, the National Credit Framework describes a master’s degree as a two-year/four-semester programme. It also recognizes a one-year/two-semester master’s route after a four-year bachelor’s degree (Honours/Research). Availability of the one-year route is university- and programme-specific.
Distance/ODL arrangements may allow a longer completion window. For example, IGNOU specifies a minimum of 24 months and maximum of 48 months for its MA (Urban Studies).
bullets: Usual route after a three-year bachelor’s degree: two years/four semesters.
Possible NEP-linked route after a four-year bachelor’s degree: one year/two semesters, only where offered.
Always check the institution’s maximum completion period, especially for distance learning.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: There is no single national MA syllabus across Arts, Humanities, Social Sciences, languages, and fine-arts disciplines: the curriculum follows the chosen subject and the awarding university’s regulations. A student should compare the current course structure, electives, methods training, assessment pattern, language of instruction, and dissertation/project options before applying.

Delhi University’s MA History provides a useful illustration of a subject MA structure: it is a two-year, four-semester programme requiring 80 credits, combining core courses, electives, open electives, and later specialization choices. This example should not be treated as the template for every MA subject or university.
bullets: Expect subject specialization rather than a generic ‘MA’ curriculum.
Look for core papers, discipline-specific electives, open/interdisciplinary electives, and research or project work where applicable.
Check the latest departmental syllabus because paper titles and credit structures can change.

sections.heading: How admission works
paragraphs: Admission processes are set by each university. A current central-university example is the University of Delhi’s 2026–27 two-year postgraduate admission process: applicants had to take the relevant CUET-PG 2026 test paper, meet the programme-specific eligibility published in the PG Bulletin, and apply through the university’s CSAS(PG) portal for seat allocation.

This is an example, not a universal MA rule. Some universities may use CUET-PG, while others may use their own entrance test, merit criteria, interviews, or a separate distance-learning admission process. Application dates, fees, test-paper mappings, and seat-allocation rules must be checked in the current institutional notification.
bullets: Identify the university’s admission mode before registering for an entrance examination.
Match the intended MA subject with the required test paper and published eligibility, where CUET-PG is used.
Keep track of both the examination application and the university counselling/allocation application; they can be separate steps.

sections.heading: Fees and other costs
paragraphs: MA fees vary substantially by university, delivery mode, subject, student category, and residence status; candidates should rely on the current prospectus and fee notice of the chosen institution. As a dated illustration, IGNOU lists MA (Urban Studies) at ₹8,200 per year plus ₹300 registration, and states that examination fees are additional.

Applicants should budget beyond tuition: application and entrance-test charges, registration, examination fees, study materials or internet access for online/ODL programmes, accommodation and living costs for campus programmes, and travel where applicable.
bullets: Do not generalize fees from one MA subject or university to another.
Check whether the listed programme fee includes examination, registration, and other compulsory charges.
Use the latest fee notice because charges can change by admission cycle.

sections.heading: Options after an MA
paragraphs: A relevant MA can provide the master’s-level academic route to PhD admission. Under UGC’s 2022 PhD regulations, candidates with a two-year/four-semester master’s after a three-year bachelor’s degree, or a one-year/two-semester master’s after a four-year bachelor’s degree, are eligible to seek PhD admission with at least 55% aggregate or an equivalent grade, subject to applicable relaxations and the admitting institution’s process.

UGC-NET is a major next step for candidates seeking Junior Research Fellowship, Assistant Professor eligibility, or PhD admission pathways. The June 2026 bulletin states that master’s candidates with 55% in the General/Unreserved/General-EWS categories, or 50% for OBC-NCL/SC/ST/PwD/PwBD/Third Gender categories, may be eligible; students awaiting final master’s results may apply provisionally under the stated conditions.

For civil-services preparation, an MA is an additional qualification rather than the minimum gateway: UPSC’s Civil Services Examination 2026 notice requires a graduate degree or equivalent. An MA may nevertheless support optional-subject depth, writing, and research skills, while eligibility and selection remain governed by the examination rules.
bullets: PhD in Arts, Humanities, or Social Sciences, subject to university admissions and the UGC eligibility framework.
UGC-NET for JRF, Assistant Professor eligibility, and/or PhD-admission categories.
Competitive examinations such as UPSC Civil Services and relevant State PSC examinations, subject to each notification’s eligibility rules.

Roles where subject knowledge, research, communication, and writing are useful—such as research assistance, educational-content development, and content writing—subject to employer-specific hiring criteria.
